title: Citizen Science
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Citizen science involves ordinary people participating (0) in scientific research by collecting and analysing data. This collaborative approach has become increasingly popular as technology makes it easier for non-scientists to contribute meaningfully to research projects.||Citizen science projects cover (9) a wide range of topics, from astronomy and climate change to wildlife monitoring and medical research. Participants might count birds in their gardens, classify galaxies through online platforms, or track pollution levels in their neighbourhoods. The data collected by thousands (10) of volunteers can help scientists tackle research questions that would (11) be impossible to address alone due to the scale of observation required.||One of the most successful citizen science projects is eBird, (12) which allows birdwatchers worldwide to submit their observations to a central database. This has created an enormous dataset that scientists rely (13) on to track bird populations and migration patterns. Another project, Foldit, turns protein folding into a puzzle game, helping researchers understand diseases.||Citizen science benefits both researchers and participants. Scientists gain access (14) to data on scales they couldn't achieve otherwise, while citizens develop scientific literacy and feel connected to research. Critics worry about data quality, but studies have shown that (15) when properly designed, citizen science projects can produce reliable results. (16) As technology continues to advance, citizen science will likely play an increasingly important role in scientific discovery.

example.text: Citizen science involves ordinary people participating (0) scientific research.

questions.explanation: Expression 'cover a wide range' indicates a wide variety of topics.
Expression 'thousands of volunteers' indicates a large number of people.
Conditional structure: 'would be impossible' expresses a hypothesis.
Relative pronoun referring to the eBird project.
Part of the phrasal verb 'rely on', meaning to depend on something.
Expression 'gain access to' means to obtain access to something.
Temporal conjunction that introduces the condition for reliable results.
